name: Emperor Penguin
habitat: Antarctic sea ice and open ocean
funFact: Males balance a single egg on their feet and fast for up to 115 days through the Antarctic winter to incubate it.

name: King Penguin
range: Sub-Antarctic islands including South Georgia, Crozet, Kerguelen
funFact: King penguins can dive to depths exceeding 300 meters and hold their breath for over 9 minutes.

name: Chinstrap Penguin
habitat: Icebergs, pack ice, rocky Antarctic and sub-Antarctic islands
range: Antarctic Peninsula and South Atlantic islands
funFact: Chinstrap penguins are among the most aggressive penguin species and will fight vigorously to defend their pebble nests.
threats: Predation by leopard seals and skuas

name: Adelie Penguin
habitat: Antarctic coastline, pack ice, rocky islands
range: Entire Antarctic continent and nearby islands
funFact: Adelie penguins migrate up to 17,600 km round-trip each year, one of the longest migrations of any bird.
threats: Sea ice changes from climate warming

name: Gentoo Penguin
habitat: Sub-Antarctic and Antarctic Peninsula beaches and grasslands
range: Antarctic Peninsula, Falkland Islands, South Georgia, sub-Antarctic islands
funFact: Gentoo penguins are the fastest swimming birds on Earth, reaching speeds of up to 36 km/h underwater.
threats: Predation by sea lions and leopard seals

name: Macaroni Penguin
funFact: Despite laying two eggs, Macaroni penguins almost always raise only one chick; the first, smaller egg is typically abandoned.

name: Rockhopper Penguin
habitat: Rocky coastal cliffs and slopes on sub-Antarctic islands
range: Falkland Islands, Tristan da Cunha, southern Chile, sub-Antarctic islands
funFact: Rockhopper penguins navigate steep sea cliffs by jumping with both feet together, which is unique among penguin species.
threats: Commercial fishing reducing krill and squid

name: Little Penguin
habitat: Coastal burrows in sand dunes, under vegetation, rock crevices
funFact: Little penguins are strictly nocturnal on land, returning to their burrows only after dark to avoid aerial predators.

name: African Penguin
habitat: Rocky coastal islands and mainland beaches of southern Africa
funFact: African penguins produce a braying call remarkably similar to a donkey, earning them the nickname 'jackass penguin'.
threats: Overfishing depleting anchovy and sardine stocks
Habitat loss and human disturbance at colonies

name: Magellanic Penguin
habitat: Patagonian steppe, coastal burrows, rocky and sandy beaches
range: Argentina, Chile, Falkland Islands; winters north along South American coasts
funFact: Magellanic penguins are highly faithful to their mates and nesting burrows, returning to the same site year after year.
threats: Climate change shifting prey distribution northward

name: Humboldt Penguin
habitat: Rocky and sandy coasts along the cold Humboldt Current
range: Pacific coasts of Peru and Chile
funFact: Humboldt penguins nest in burrows excavated from guano deposits, and historic guano harvesting for fertilizer devastated many colonies.

name: Galapagos Penguin
habitat: Rocky lava coastlines and cool upwelling zones
range: Galapagos Islands, Ecuador (primarily Fernandina and Isabela)
breedingSeason: Variable; triggered by cool water temperatures
funFact: The Galapagos penguin is the only penguin species that regularly lives and breeds north of the equator.
threats: El Niño events drastically reducing food availability

name: Yellow-eyed Penguin
habitat: Coastal temperate forest and scrubland; nests among tree roots and dense vegetation
range: New Zealand South Island, Stewart Island, Auckland Islands, Campbell Island
diet: Blue cod, opal fish, sprat, squid
funFact: Yellow-eyed penguins are highly solitary nesters and require visual isolation from other pairs; they rarely breed in colonies.

name: Fiordland Penguin
habitat: Temperate rainforest coastal areas; nests in caves and under tree roots
range: Fiordland and Stewart Island, New Zealand
funFact: Fiordland penguins are exceptionally secretive breeders, nesting deep in dense rainforest and rarely seen by humans during the breeding season.
threats: Stoat and weasel predation on chicks and eggs

name: Snares Penguin
habitat: Dense Olearia forest and rocky shores on predator-free island
funFact: Snares penguins breed exclusively on The Snares archipelago, one of the smallest and most restricted ranges of any penguin species.
threats: Limited range making entire population vulnerable to single events

name: Erect-crested Penguin
habitat: Rocky shores and tussock grassland on remote sub-Antarctic islands
range: Bounty Islands and Antipodes Islands, New Zealand
funFact: Erect-crested penguins can raise and lower their yellow crest feathers voluntarily, which no other crested penguin species can do.
threats: Population has declined over 70% since the 1970s for poorly understood reasons

name: Royal Penguin
habitat: Sandy and pebble beaches and slopes of a single sub-Antarctic island
funFact: Royal penguins nest in one of the largest penguin colonies on Earth, with some individual colonies numbering over 500,000 birds.
threats: Entire world population restricted to a single island

name: White-flippered Penguin
habitat: Rocky coastal areas with burrows in limestone cliffs and boulders
range: Banks Peninsula and Motunau Island, Canterbury, New Zealand
funFact: The white-flippered penguin is distinguished by the broad white margins on both edges of its flippers, unlike the plain dark flippers of its closest relative.
